<math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4.8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>

<math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4.8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>

<math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4.8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>
solution:
Given magnetic field, B→=1.6×10-6cos2×107z+6×1015t2i^+j^Wbm2
The relation between magnetic field and electric field is E=cB=(3×108)×(1.6×10-6×5)=4.8×102×5
The direction of light propagation will be along E→×B→ .
E→=4.8×102cos2×107z+6×1015t-i^+2j^Vm

The instantaneous force experienced by the particle is:
question: The electric fields of two plane electromagnetic plane waves in vacuum are given by <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> At <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> a particle of charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> is at origin with a velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>08</mn><mi>c</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> ( <math><mi>c</mi></math> is the speed of light in vaccum). The instantaneous force experienced by the particle is:
solution:
Magnetic field vectors associated with this electromagnetic wave are given by
B→1=E0ck^cos⁡kx-ωt & B→2=E0ci^cos⁡ky-ωt
F→=qE→+qV→×B→
=qE→1+E→2+qV→×B→1+B→2
By putting the value of E→1,E→2,B→1&B→2
The net Lorentz force on the charged particles is
F→=qE00.8cos⁡kx-ωti^+cosky-ωtj^+0.2cosky-ωtk^
At t=0 and at x=y=0
F→=qE00.8i^+j^+0.2k^

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic source of light operating at $15$ kW emits $2.5\times 10^{22}$ photons/s. The region of an electromagnetic spectrum to which the emitted electromagnetic radiation belongs to ________. (Take $h=6.6\times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c=3\times 10^8$ m/s).
question: A monochromatic source of light operating at $15$ kW emits $2.5\times 10^{22}$ photons/s. The region of an electromagnetic spectrum to which the emitted electromagnetic radiation belongs to ________. (Take $h=6.6\times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c=3\times 10^8$ m/s).
solution:
Power of the source, $P = 15 \text{ kW} = 15 \times 10^3 \text{ J/s}$

Number of photons emitted per second, $n = 2.5 \times 10^{22} \text{ s}^{-1}$

Energy of one photon, $E = \dfrac{P}{n} = \dfrac{15 \times 10^3}{2.5 \times 10^{22}} = 6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Using $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$, we get $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 330 \text{ nm}$

The wavelength $330 \text{ nm}$ lies in the ultraviolet region of the electromagnetic spectrum.

Answer: Ultraviolet

subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave is propagating along the direction <math><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> with its polarization along the direction <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> The correct form of the magnetic field of the wave would be (here <math><msub><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> is an appropriate constant):
question: A plane electromagnetic wave is propagating along the direction <math><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> with its polarization along the direction <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> The correct form of the magnetic field of the wave would be (here <math><msub><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> is an appropriate constant):
solution:
Polarization of EM Wave means Direction of Its electric Field

Electric field vector is along k^

So, unit electric filed vector, E→=k^ (Given)

Direction of wave,c^=i^+j^2  (Given)

B^  denotes unit magnetic field vector.

B^=C^ ×E^

B^=i^+j^2×k^=i^-j^2

General Form is B=(B0B^)cosωt-c^k

where B0 is Magnitude of Magnetic Field

So, B=B0i^-j^2cosωt-ki^+j^2

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave travels in free space along the $x$-direction. At a particular point in space and time, $\vec{B} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \hat{j}$ T is associated with this wave. The value of corresponding electric field $\vec{E}$ at this point is _______ V/m.
question: An electromagnetic wave travels in free space along the $x$-direction. At a particular point in space and time, $\vec{B} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \hat{j}$ T is associated with this wave. The value of corresponding electric field $\vec{E}$ at this point is _______ V/m.
solution:
The direction of propagation of an electromagnetic wave is given by the direction of $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$.

Given that the wave travels along the $x$-direction, the direction of propagation is $\hat{i}$.

The magnetic field is given as $\vec{B} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \hat{j}$ T, so its direction is $\hat{j}$.

Let the direction of the electric field be $\hat{n}$. We must have:

$\hat{n} \times \hat{j} = \hat{i}$

Since $(-\hat{k}) \times \hat{j} = \hat{i}$, the direction of the electric field is $-\hat{k}$.

The magnitude of the electric field is related to the magnetic field by $E = cB$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light in free space.

$E = (3 \times 10^8) \times (2 \times 10^{-7}) = 60$ V/m

Therefore, the electric field vector is $\vec{E} = -60 \hat{k}$ V/m.

Answer: $-60 \hat{k}$

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of average electric energy density and total average energy density of electromagnetic wave is:
question: The ratio of average electric energy density and total average energy density of electromagnetic wave is:
solution:
Average electric energy density is stated as uE=12ε0E2

Total average energy density U= 12ε0E2+12μ0B2

=12ε0E2+12μ0E2c2    (As B=Ec)

=12ε0E2+12μ0E2ε0μ0=12ε0E2+12E2ε0=ε0E2

Hence, the ratio, uEU=12.

subject: Physics
q: A plane polarized monochromatic EM wave is travelling a vacuum along $z$ direction such that at $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_1$ it is found that the electric field is zero at a spatial point $z_1$. The next zero that occurs in its neighbourhood is at $z_2$. The frequency of the electromagnetic wave is:
question: A plane polarized monochromatic EM wave is travelling a vacuum along $z$ direction such that at $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_1$ it is found that the electric field is zero at a spatial point $z_1$. The next zero that occurs in its neighbourhood is at $z_2$. The frequency of the electromagnetic wave is:
solution:
Using $E=E_0-e^i(k z-\omega t)$
Given, at $t=t_1, z=z_1, E=0$
the next zero that occurs in it's neighborhood is at $z_2$, the frequency of the electromagnetic wave at $t_2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&e^{i\left(k z_1-\omega t_1\right)}=e^{i\left(k z_2-\omega t_2\right)} \\
&k z_1-\omega t_1=k z_2-\omega t_2 \\
&\left(t_2-t_1\right) \omega=k\left(z-z_1\right) \\
&\text { where } k=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=2 \pi v \\
&\left(t_2-t_1\right)=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda \times 2 \pi v}\left(z_2-z_1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\quad=\frac{1}{x \times v}\left(z_2-z_1\right) \\
&\Rightarrow \lambda \times v=\frac{\left(z_2-z_1\right)}{\left(t_2-t_1\right)}=C \\
&\quad\left(t_2-t_1\right)=\frac{\left(z_2-z_1\right)}{C} \\
&\text { Frequency is } f \propto \frac{1}{t} \text { then } \frac{1}{\left(t_2-t_1\right)}=\frac{C}{\left(z_2-z_1\right)} \\
&\therefore \text { Frequency, } f=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{\left(z_2-z_1\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

q: An electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math>, is travelling in a medium whose relative electric permittivity and relative magnetic permeability both are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Its velocity in this medium is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math>, is travelling in a medium whose relative electric permittivity and relative magnetic permeability both are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Its velocity in this medium is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Given : Frequency of wave f=5GHz

=5×109 Hz

Relative permittivity, ϵr=2 and Relative permeability, μr=2 Since speed of light in a medium is given by,

v=1μ∈=1μrμ0·ϵr∈0

v=1μrϵf1μ0ϵ0=Cμrϵr

Where C is speed of light is vacuum

∴ v=3×1084=30×1072 m s-1

=15×107 m s-1

∴ Ans. is 15

subject: Physics
q: In the given electromagnetic wave $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{y}}=600 \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{kx}) \mathrm{Vm}^{-1}$, intensity of the associated light beam is (in $\mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^2$ : (Given $\epsilon_0=9 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
question: In the given electromagnetic wave $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{y}}=600 \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{kx}) \mathrm{Vm}^{-1}$, intensity of the associated light beam is (in $\mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^2$ : (Given $\epsilon_0=9 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Intensity } & =\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{E}_0^2 \mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-12} \times(600)^2 \times 3 \times 10^8 \\ & =\frac{9}{2} \times 36 \times 3=486 \mathrm{w} / \mathrm{m}^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: A time varying electric field is a source of changing magnetic field and vice-versa. Thus a disturbance in electric or magnetic field creates EM waves.

Statement II: In a material medium, the EM wave travels with speed v=1μ0ϵ0.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: A time varying electric field is a source of changing magnetic field and vice-versa. Thus a disturbance in electric or magnetic field creates EM waves.

Statement II: In a material medium, the EM wave travels with speed v=1μ0ϵ0.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both statement I and statement II are true
Both statement I and statement II are false
Statement I is correct but statement II is false
Statement I is incorrect but statement II is true
solution:
According to the Maxwell’s EM theory, the EM waves propagation contains electric and magnetic field vibration in mutually perpendicular direction. Thus, the changing of electric field give rise to magnetic field.

Also, time varying magnetic field produces electric field. According to Faradays law, when magnetic flux changes in the region surrounded by conductor, it produces electric field (induced emf) in conductor. 

In a material medium the EM wave travels with speed given by v=1μεv=1μ0μr(ε0εr)

Thus, statement I is correct but statement II is false.

q: During the propagation of electromagnetic wave in a particular medium :-
question: During the propagation of electromagnetic wave in a particular medium :-
options: Electric energy density is double of the magnetic energy density
Electric energy density is half of the magnetic energy density
Electric energy density is equal to the magnetic energy density
Both electric and magnetic energy densities are zero
solution:
UVelec=12∈0E2      ....(i)

UVmag=12B2/μ0     ....(ii)

B = E C  (for EMW)         ....(iii)

and C= 1 μ 0 ε 0 .....(iv)

∴ devide eqn (i) by (ii) 

(U/V)elec(U/V)mag=12ε0E212B2/μ0

= ( E B ) 2 μ 0 ε 0

by using eq (iii) & (iv)

= C 2 C 2 =1

⇒(U/V)elec.=(UV)mag

subject: Physics
q:
The ratio of speeds of electromagnetic waves in vacuum and a medium, having dielectric constant $k=3$ and permeability of $\mu=2 \mu_{0}$, is
($\mu_{0}=$ permeability of vacuum)
question:
The ratio of speeds of electromagnetic waves in vacuum and a medium, having dielectric constant $k=3$ and permeability of $\mu=2 \mu_{0}$, is
($\mu_{0}=$ permeability of vacuum)
solution:
Speed of electromagnetic wave in vacuum: $c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}$
Speed in a medium with permeability $\mu = 2\mu_0$ and dielectric constant $k = 3$:
$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \epsilon}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\mu_0 \times 3\epsilon_0}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6\mu_0\epsilon_0}}$
$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\epsilon_0}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{6}}$
Ratio of speeds: $\frac{c}{v} = \sqrt{6}$
Therefore: $c : v = \sqrt{6} : 1$

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave of frequency $v=3.0 \mathrm{MHz}$ passes from vacuum into a dielectric medium with permittivity $\varepsilon=4.0$. Then
question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency $v=3.0 \mathrm{MHz}$ passes from vacuum into a dielectric medium with permittivity $\varepsilon=4.0$. Then
options: wavelength is doubled and the frequency remains unchanged
wavelength is doubled and frequency becomes half
wavelength is halved and frequency remains unchanged
wavelength and frequency both remain unchanged.
solution:
Refractive index, $\mu=\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0}}=2$
Speed and wavelength of wave will becomes half, the frequency remaining unchanged (frequency of a wave depends on the source as due to refraction, it is assumed that the energy is conserved. hv remains the same)

subject: Physics
q:
The equation of the electric field of an electromagnetic wave propagating through free space is given by : $E=\sqrt{377} \sin \left(6.27 \times 10^{3} t-2.09 \times 10^{-5} x\right) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{C}$
The average power of the electromagnetic wave is $\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}\right) \mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$
(Take $\sqrt{\frac{\mu_{0}}{\varepsilon_{o}}}=377$ in SI units)
question:
The equation of the electric field of an electromagnetic wave propagating through free space is given by : $E=\sqrt{377} \sin \left(6.27 \times 10^{3} t-2.09 \times 10^{-5} x\right) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{C}$
The average power of the electromagnetic wave is $\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}\right) \mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$
(Take $\sqrt{\frac{\mu_{0}}{\varepsilon_{o}}}=377$ in SI units)
solution:
From the electric field equation, amplitude:
$E_0 = \sqrt{377}$ N/C
Average intensity of EM wave:
$I_{avg} = \frac{E_0^2}{2Z_0}$ where $Z_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 377$ Ω
$I_{avg} = \frac{(\sqrt{377})^2}{2 \times 377} = \frac{377}{2 \times 377} = \frac{1}{2}$ W/m²
Given $I_{avg} = \frac{1}{\alpha}$ W/m², we have $\frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{2}$
Therefore, $\alpha = 2$

subject: Physics
q: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave, travelling in an unknown nonmagnetic medium is given by, $E_{\mathrm{y}}=20 \sin \left(3 \times 10^{6} x-4.5 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$ (where $x, \mathrm{t}$ and other values have S.I. units). The dielectric constant of the medium is $\_\_\_\_$ (speed of light in free space is $3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$)
question: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave, travelling in an unknown nonmagnetic medium is given by, $E_{\mathrm{y}}=20 \sin \left(3 \times 10^{6} x-4.5 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$ (where $x, \mathrm{t}$ and other values have S.I. units). The dielectric constant of the medium is $\_\_\_\_$ (speed of light in free space is $3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$)
solution:
From the wave equation $E_y = 20\sin(3 \times 10^6 x - 4.5 \times 10^{14} t)$:
Wave number: $k = 3 \times 10^6$ m⁻¹
Angular frequency: $\omega = 4.5 \times 10^{14}$ rad/s
Speed of wave in medium: $v = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{14}}{3 \times 10^6} = 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s
Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
Refractive index: $n = \frac{c}{v} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^8} = 2$
For a nonmagnetic medium: $n = \sqrt{\epsilon_r}$
Dielectric constant: $\epsilon_r = n^2 = 4$

subject: Physics
q: Sunlight of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math>is incident normally on the surface of a solar panel. Some part of incident energy (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math>) is reflected from the surface and the rest is absorbed. The force exerted on <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> surface area will be close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: Sunlight of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math>is incident normally on the surface of a solar panel. Some part of incident energy (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math>) is reflected from the surface and the rest is absorbed. The force exerted on <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> surface area will be close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
The pressure exerted by the photons

 P=0.75×Ic +0.25×2Ic ( P = pressure, c = speed of light, I = intensity )

P=1.25 ×Ic

 We know the force can be computed using

F=P×A 

( F = force, P = pressure, A = area )

F=1.25×50×13×108

F=20.83×10-8 N

subject: Physics
q: Photons of an electromagnetic radiation has an energy $11 \mathrm{keV}$ each. To which region of electromagnetic spectrum does it belong ?
question: Photons of an electromagnetic radiation has an energy $11 \mathrm{keV}$ each. To which region of electromagnetic spectrum does it belong ?
solution:
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda} \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{E}}$ $\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{11 \times 1000 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$ $=12.4 Å$
wavelength range of visible region is $4000 Å$ to $7800 Å$

subject: Physics
q:
Match List - I with List - II.
$\begin{array}{l} \text{List - I} & \text{List - II} \\ \text{Relation} & \text{Law} \\ \text{A. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=-\frac{d}{d t} \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a} & \text{I. Ampere's circuital law} \\ \text{B. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0}\left(I+\epsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}\right) & \text{II. Faraday's laws of} \\ & \text{electromagnetic induction} \\ \text{C. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \int_{\mathrm{V}} \rho \mathrm{dv} & \text{III. Ampere - Maxwell law} \\ \text{D. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I & \text{IV. Gauss's law of electrostatics} \end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List - I with List - II.
$\begin{array}{l} \text{List - I} & \text{List - II} \\ \text{Relation} & \text{Law} \\ \text{A. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=-\frac{d}{d t} \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a} & \text{I. Ampere's circuital law} \\ \text{B. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0}\left(I+\epsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}\right) & \text{II. Faraday's laws of} \\ & \text{electromagnetic induction} \\ \text{C. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \int_{\mathrm{V}} \rho \mathrm{dv} & \text{III. Ampere - Maxwell law} \\ \text{D. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I & \text{IV. Gauss's law of electrostatics} \end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Theoretical
A-II, B-III, C-IV, D-I

A. $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt} \oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{a}$ → Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction (II)
B. $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 \left(I + \epsilon_0 \frac{d\Phi_E}{dt}\right)$ → Ampere-Maxwell law (III)
C. $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{a} = \frac{1}{\epsilon_0} \int \rho \, dv$ → Gauss's law of electrostatics (IV)
D. $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I$ → Ampere's circuital law (I)

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following Maxwell’s equation is valid for time varying conditions but not valid for static conditions:
question: Which of the following Maxwell’s equation is valid for time varying conditions but not valid for static conditions:
solution:
Maxwell’s four equations describe the electric and magnetic fields arising from distributions of electric charges and currents, and how those fields change in time.

The equations are as follows:

∮E→·dA→=qε0   ...1∮B⇀·dA⇀=0   ...2∮E⇀·dl⇀=-∂ϕB∂t   ...3∮B⇀·dl⇀=μ0I   ...4

From the above set of equations, it is clear that only the third equation is valid for time varying field.

Hence, this is the correct option.

(given ϵ0=8.8×10-12C2 N-1 m-2)

5.5×10-12=12×8.8×10-12×502×V
V=500 cm3

subject: Physics
q: The electric field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by, E=50sin500x-10×1010t V m-1. The velocity of an electromagnetic wave in this medium is: (Given c= the speed of light in vacuum).
question: The electric field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by, E=50sin500x-10×1010t V m-1. The velocity of an electromagnetic wave in this medium is: (Given c= the speed of light in vacuum).
solution:
The equation of electric field is given in the question, E=50sin500x-10×1010t V m-1 now compare it the equation of electric field in an electromagnetic wave, E=E0sinkx-ωt V m-1

⇒ω=1010 rad s-1 and, k=50 m-1, so the velocity of electromagnetic waves is, v=ωk=101050=2×108=23c.

q: The oscillating magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>y</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>1000</mn><mi>π</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The amplitude of electric field will be
question: The oscillating magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>y</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>1000</mn><mi>π</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The amplitude of electric field will be
solution:
Given here, By=5×10-6sin1000π5x-4×108t T

Here, peak value of magnetic field, B0=5×10-6 T

Now the speed of the wave v=ωk=1000π×4×1081000π×5=8×107 m s-1

Using relation between magnetic and electric field, 

Peak value of electric field is E0=B0v=5×10-6×8×107=4×102 V m-1

subject: Physics
q: Electromagnetic waves travel in a medium with speed of $1.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The relative permeability of the medium is 2.0 . The relative permittivity will be:
question: Electromagnetic waves travel in a medium with speed of $1.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The relative permeability of the medium is 2.0 . The relative permittivity will be:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{m}} \times \mu_{\mathrm{m}}}{\varepsilon_0 \times \mu_0}=\frac{\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}^2}}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}^2}} \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \times \mu_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{c}^2}{\mathrm{v}^2} \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \times 2=\frac{\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2}{\left(1.5 \times 10^8\right)^2} \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \times 2=4 \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : EM waves used for optical communication have longer wavelengths than that of microwave, employed in Radar technology.

Reason R : Infrared EM waves are more energetic than microwaves, (used in Radar)

In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : EM waves used for optical communication have longer wavelengths than that of microwave, employed in Radar technology.

Reason R : Infrared EM waves are more energetic than microwaves, (used in Radar)

In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
An optical telecommunication is communication at a distance using light to carry information. It can be performed visually or by using electronic devices.

The wavelength of the microwaves are greater than that of the common optical light waves.

Hence, the Assertion is not correct. But, because of the small wavelength, the electromagnetic waves are more energetic than the microwaves. Hence, the reason is true.

subject: Physics
q: A light beam is described by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>800</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>c</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> An electron is allowed to move normal to the propagation of light beam with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is the maximum magnetic force exerted on the electron?
question: A light beam is described by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>800</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>c</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> An electron is allowed to move normal to the propagation of light beam with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is the maximum magnetic force exerted on the electron?
solution:
E0=800 V

B0=E0c=8003×108

Fm=qvB0=1.6×10-19×3×107×8003×108

=12.8×10-18 N
